title: Graduating into an AI World: Finding First Jobs Today
content:
Entering the workforce is proving to be exceptionally tough for recent college graduates in the current economy. The United States is experiencing a low-hire environment where big employers are laying off workers in droves and job openings have dropped significantly. To make matters worse, artificial intelligence is radically transforming and potentially destroying entry-level white-collar work. The unemployment rate for recent college graduates has grown higher than the national average for all workers. Consequently, underemployment for recent grads reached almost 43 percent by the end of last year.

The difficulty is compounded by a fundamental mismatch between what students are studying and where the economy is creating jobs. According to labor market data, college completions have outpaced the growth of suitable entry-level positions over the last two decades. In many popular fields of study, the ratio of entry-level jobs per graduate has sharply declined. Conversely, fields like health care have plenty of openings but a slow growth in qualified graduates. Economists argue that a higher education system that fails to tailor curriculums to employer demands is a major impediment.

Finding a job today feels like traversing a treacherous obstacle course of AI application screening systems and hesitant hiring managers. The rapid improvement in generative AI tools has caused many employers to pause their junior hiring efforts entirely. Furthermore, in a tight job market, companies often prefer to fill junior roles with more experienced candidates rather than taking a chance on new grads. This forces young professionals to aggressively pursue multiple avenues and consider career paths they had never originally planned. Ultimately, the education pathway to jobs appears to be fundamentally broken for this generation.
